coredata.dc:title: Achieving overall rejection of pharmaceuticals and personal care products of different polarities by controlling interlayer charging environment of graphene oxide membrane using carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Oikawa, Mutsuki
Takeuchi, Haruka
Koide, Ryota
Yoshizawa, Noriko
Wang, Zheng-Ming
Koura, Setsuko
coredata.dc:description: To realize overall rejection of numerous pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) with varieties in hydrophilicity (dissolubility giving either negative or positive charges and non-dissolubility) and hydrophobicity, a diverse interlayer charging environment was controlled by intercalating two-dimensional carbon quantum dot (CQD)-like nanoparticles into graphene oxide membrane (GOM). It was found that ethylenediamine-added sucrose-derived CQD with a positively charged surface in aqueous solution is appropriate for such a purpose because of the strong electrostatic attraction by the graphene oxide (GO) surface of opposite charge. The CQD contains plenty of pyridine groups of stronger basicity and pyrrole groups of weaker basicity, which contribute majorly to anchoring the CQD onto GO surface and to donating positive charges in GOM interlayer, respectively. This unique membrane design strategy allows coexisting of both negatively and positively charged groups in a slightly expanded hydrophilic interlayer space of GOM. As the result, not only rejections were promoted for the negatively dissociative PPCPs (from 77 % to all more than 86%) and neutral PPCPs (from 25 % to all more than 56%) but also rejections of the positively dissociative PPCPs became remarkable (from null to all more than 56%). The research result provides a promising methodology of changing chemical environment of a simpler membrane material other than nanofiltration or reverse osmosis to achieve an all-together rejecting effect toward PPCPs of various polarities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide membrane
Carbon quantum dot
PPCPs
Overall rejection
Interlayer charging environment

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: XRD, SEM and TEM of CQD-intercalated GOM
RAMAN, FT-IR, and XPS of CQD-intercalated GOM

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by a JSPS KAKENHI Grant (22K18043) from the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) and partially by the Tsukuba Innovation Arena for Nanotechnology (TIA-nano) Kakehashi program. M. O. and R. K. conducted this work under the professorship of partner graduate school between National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST) and Chiba Institute of Technology (CIT) and are indebted to AIST for technical assistance and financial support.

This work was financially supported by a JSPS KAKENHI Grant (22K18043) from the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) and partially by the Tsukuba Innovation Arena for Nanotechnology (TIA-nano) Kakehashi program. M. O. and R. K. conducted this work under the professorship of partner graduate school between National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST) and Chiba Institute of Technology (CIT) and are indebted to AIST for technical assistance and financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic description on formation of the CQD-intercalated layered composite GOM.

(a) Zeta-potentials of GO (●), Suc(3 h)-CP (△), and EDA(2 h) (○)-, NH3 (3 h) (□)-, L-Cys(3 h) (▲)-, Pipd(3 h) (◊)-, and NaOH(3 h) (+)-CPs at different pH, (b) comparison of FT-IR spectra of sucrose (yellow), Suc-CQD (blue), and EDA-CQD (green), (c) UV–Vis spectra of Suc-CQD (black) and EDA-CQD (red), and PL spectrum of EDA-CQD (green) at the excitation of 390 nm, and (d) excitation-emission-matrix (EEM) spectrum of EDA-CQD. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

XRD patterns of (a) dried and (b) wet GOM (yellow), EDA_GOM (green), and EDA-CQD_GOM (grey) and (c) a schematic description on the changes of their interlayer distances before and after drying. The values in (a) and (b) are the d001 values and those by the arrows in (c) show the change of d001 values before and after drying (Δd001 ). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
FE-SEM images from the top surface of (a) GOM and (b ∼ f) EDA-CQD_GOM, and (g) a cross-sectional SEM image of the self-supporting EDA-CQD_GOM with a greater thickness.
Top view TEM images of (a, b) EDA-CQD_GOM, the corresponding high-resolution expanded ones, and (c) GOM.

(a) Raman spectra of EDA(3 h)-CP (black), GOM (orange), and EDA-CQD_GOM (green), (b) FT-IR spectra of GOM (orange), EDA_GOM (blue), and EDA-CQD_GOM (green), and (c) C 1 s and (d) N 1 s XPS of GO, Suc-CQD, EDA-CQD, and EDA-CQD_GOM. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Changes in rejections of MO (orange) and MB (blue) and the corresponding water fluxes after separation operation for 2 (shaded square) and 24 (filled square) h. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Rejections of negatively charged (○), neutral (◊), and positively charged (□) PPCPs on (a) pure GOM and (b) EDA-CQD_GOM and (c) columnar comparison of rejections by PPCPs’ kind on pure GOM (●) and EDA-CQD_GOM (●). The number in parenthesis after the PPCPs’ name in (c) is molecular weight (g･mol−1). Data are not plotted in (a) and (c) for positively charged compounds that showed negative removal rates.

Proportional ratios of rejections of (a) EDA-CQD_GOM, (b) MB0.5-GOM, and (c) MB1-GOM over that of GOM for removals of negatively charged (○), neutral (◊), and positively charged (□) PPCPs. The right-side photographs are images of GOM, EDA-CQD_GOM, MB0.5_GOM and MB1_GOM before separation experiment from top to bottom.

head.ce:title: Achieving overall rejection of pharmaceuticals and personal care products of different polarities by controlling interlayer charging environment of graphene oxide membrane using carbon quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Global Zero Emission Research Center, National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology, 16-1 Onogawa, Tsukuba, Ibaraki 305-8569, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To realize overall rejection of numerous pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) with varieties in hydrophilicity (dissolubility giving either negative or positive charges and non-dissolubility) and hydrophobicity, a diverse interlayer charging environment was controlled by intercalating two-dimensional carbon quantum dot (CQD)-like nanoparticles into graphene oxide membrane (GOM). It was found that ethylenediamine-added sucrose-derived CQD with a positively charged surface in aqueous solution is appropriate for such a purpose because of the strong electrostatic attraction by the graphene oxide (GO) surface of opposite charge. The CQD contains plenty of pyridine groups of stronger basicity and pyrrole groups of weaker basicity, which contribute majorly to anchoring the CQD onto GO surface and to donating positive charges in GOM interlayer, respectively. This unique membrane design strategy allows coexisting of both negatively and positively charged groups in a slightly expanded hydrophilic interlayer space of GOM. As the result, not only rejections were promoted for the negatively dissociative PPCPs (from 77 % to all more than 86%) and neutral PPCPs (from 25 % to all more than 56%) but also rejections of the positively dissociative PPCPs became remarkable (from null to all more than 56%). The research result provides a promising methodology of changing chemical environment of a simpler membrane material other than nanofiltration or reverse osmosis to achieve an all-together rejecting effect toward PPCPs of various polarities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide membrane (GOM) has recently attracted considerable attention because of the extraordinary selectivity in the permeation of water or water vapor and the rejection of the penetration of smaller molecules such as helium and hydrogen [1–12]. GOM has merits in chemical resistance, anti-fouling ability, and economic cost as a carbon-based membrane material. It can be used itself or as an active modifier layer for desalination and various water treatment applications [13–22]. While desalination (the removal or rejection of typical salt ions such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+) effect of GOM has been demonstrated in many previous reports [23,34], graphene oxide (GO) is well-known as a cation exchanger [35–40], meaning that GOM is potentially able to be penetrated by ionic species through an electrostatic force-based ion exchange process. Through a deeper insight into the peculiar separation property of GOM, it was recently elucidated that not only cationic permeation rate but also water flux are sensible with the strength of cationic power, showing a linear changing pattern but with a different increasing slope for cation series of different valences [41]. This is in principle different from other conventional membrane techniques such as nanofiltration (NF) or reverse osmosis (RO) where the physical size exclusion (partially electrostatic interaction) or solution diffusion are the main separation mechanisms [42–44].

On the other hand, detailed examination on separation of dye molecules by GOM disclosed different removal mechanisms, i.e., elctrostatic repelling and adsorption mechanisms, for negatively charged molecules such as methyl orange (MO) and positively charged molecules such as methylene blue (MB), respectively, because of the peculiar negatively charged surface property of GOM [41,45]. Adsorption mechanism increases rejections of positively charged dyes by GOM whereas it largely decreases water flux due to membrane blockade and in general disfavors repeating use because of the regeneration issue in contrast to that of elctrostatic repelling. Such a unique polarity-dependent separation behavior of GOM reminds of the advantage and necessity of a new membrane design idea based on only the electrostatic repulsion effect. That is, if an overall electrostatic repelling effect can be realized toward organic pollutants of both like and opposite charges by appropriately controlling interlayer charging environment of GOM, e.g., by creating an interlayer environment coexisting with both negatively and positively charged groups, those organic pollutants with wide varieties in polarities (dissolubility becoming negatively or positively charged and non-dissolubility) can then be persistently cut off without the cumbersome adsorption accumulation.

Because of acidic property of GO whose rich surface functional groups (SFGs) can be easily dissociated in water to show a negatively charged surface, so far a number of basic or positively dissociable intercalants (small molecules or monomers) were used to modify the interlayer of GO through acid-base or ion-exchange reaction [36,37,40]. During the traditional processes, the interlayer charging environment of GO usually changes in a manner of losing charges due to electrical neutralization by the intercalants of opposite charge or at the same time increasing hydrophobicity in many cases due to the hydrophobic moieties of the introduced intercalants. However, these methods have difficulty in increasing the oppositely charged groups and are not devoted to the creation of negatively and positively charged groups-coexisting interlayer environment. There are also other modification or synthesis strategies such as pillaring technique employing charged clusters as bridging materials [38,39], mixed matrix membrane (MMM) fabrication technique using separated GO layers to matrix larger nanoparticle fillers [46,47], and so forth. These methods, however, tend to expand interlayer distance of GOM too largely to increase exclusion opportunity of small organic pollutants. In contrast to conventional intercalants or bridging species, carbon quantum dot (CQD) is an amazing and superior candidate in terms of enabling the interlayer environment coexisting with both negative and positive charges for GOM [48,49]. This is because ① CQDs are small sized and can be strongly localized in GO interlayers if they are prepared with the surface positively charged, ② CQDs are rich of surface functional groups (SFGs) primarily at the particle edges and because of geometric constrains (the size of CQDs being equivalent of several to several tens of aromatic rings-like linkages) a lot of spare positively dissociative groups can be donated in GOM interlayer once they are fastened through a part of SFGs on the basal plane of GO, ③ a part of SFGs on GO typical of negatively charged groups can remain free due to geometric constrains even though CQDs are intercalated, and ④ CODs are usually of two-dimensional geometry, which allows only a slight expansion of interlayer distance of GOM so that small organic pollutants have less opportunity to escape from GOM while at the same time water flux can be improved. These properties indicate that CQDs-like materials are peculiar in surface chemistry and geometric structure that other simpler basic groups-contained compounds may not have. They also differ from some other polymer or biopolymer species containing lots of amine groups, e.g., untied deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) or polyethylenimine (PEI) used as a GOM modifier for pH-responsible separation [45], which either are easy to change shape or dimensions as the result of aggregates formation due to such factors as stimulus-induced conformation change, or have less varieties in the type of basic groups (or have basic groups coexisting with structural acidic groups). Thus, CQDs-like materials are the hopeful selection among the intercalant candidates capable of modifying and controlling interlayer charging environment of GOM to achieve an overall or all-together rejection effect toward organic pollutants of various polarities.

Pharmaceuticals and Personal Care Products (PPCPs) are a typical group of organic micropollutants counting more than thousands of kinds with rich varieties in polarity [50]. Their complete removal has long been expected for the purpose of higher quality water reclamation and recycling but was not fully satisfied by conventional and even advanced membrane filtration methods (refer to Supplementary Materials) [51–56]. So far CQDs have been used for various advanced water treatments. In many cases, however, they were limited only as the modifier of conventional membrane materials [57], and in a few cases they appeared to be used as the intercalant of GOM but without such a persuasive strategy as the concept of adjusting interlayer charging environment [58,59]. Herein, we attempted to design a new GOM-based hydride membrane material whose interlayer charging environment is controllable through intercalation of CQD to achieve the aforementioned removal effect. We found that a specially prepared nitrogen-contained CQD is appropriate for such a purpose. The CQD-intercalated GOM showed a “universal” rejection effect toward PPCPs of different polarities either due to electrostatic expulsion or as the result of polarity expelling (hydrophilicity-hydrophobicity incompatibility) from the interlayer surface.

Sucrose (Suc), urea (U), thiourea (TU) and 25 wt% ammonia solution (NH3) of guaranteed purity, ethylenediammonium dichloride (EDA), piperidine (Pipd) and L-cystine (L-Cys) of Wako special grade, and 1 M sodium hydroxide solution (NaOH) of volumetric analysis purity were purchased from FUJIFILM-Wako Pure Chemical Co. (Hiratsuka, Japan) and used without further purification. Parent GO sol of 4 mg∙L−1 concentration with a less than 10 μm lateral size of GO layers was purchased from Graphenea s.a. (Donostia, Spain). The VCWP09025-type nitrocellulose membrane (NCM) from Merck Co. (Rahway, USA) (pore size: 0.1 μm; diameter: 90 mm) was used as the supporting substrate membrane.

CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction of sucrose in water without or with an additive of EDA, NH3, Pipd, L-Cys, U, TU, or NaOH. To prepare the pure sucrose-derived CQD, 1.2 g of sucrose was dissolved in 12.8 mL milliQ water to form an 8.6 mass% sucrose solution. After sonification for 10 min, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined container, sealed in a stainless-still reactor, and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 448 K for 1 h. The obtained solution was then dialyzed with visking dialysis tubing (regenerated cellulose, pore size 1.25 nm, cutoff molecular weight 3500) in 100 mL milliQ water for 24 h. The outside solution containing the penetrated CQD particles, represented by Suc-CQD, was collected for further use. For the additive-added sucrose-derived CQDs, the method and condition were exactly the same as the above except for that 0.3 g of EDA, L-Cys, U, or TU were mixed in the solution or the solution pH was adjusted to 11.6 ± 0.1 using Pipd, NH3 or NaOH. The CQDs obtained with additives were represented by x-CQD where x is the abbreviated additive name. In addition, extending of the above hydrothermal reaction time to 2 ∼ 3 h gave carbon particle materials of larger size which either made it possible to measure electrophoresis behavior or can be collected by centrifugation. These samples, represented by x(t h)-CP where t stands for the hydrothermal time, were used for the convenience of surface structure characterizations of CQDs on the basis of the consideration that the similar surface functional groups remain and prefer to exist on the edges of the carbon particles nevertheless the particle size grows from that of CQDs to that of x(t)-CPs.

The CQD-intercalated GOM were fabricated by a two-step evacuation method using a Buechner funnel system in which a GOM was first deposited on a NCM substrate and subsequently a CQD solution was filtrated through the formed GOM. Typically, 500 mL of the GO solution with a concentration of 1/1600 mg∙mL−1 was first vacuum-filtrated through the supporting NCM substrate using an ordinary Buechner funnel system. After the filtration was almost completed, 100 mL of 1/10 v/v-diluted CQD solution was added, and the filtration was continued until the CQD solution completely passed through the formed GOM. During the second filtration step, the CQDs of opposite charge were retained in interlayers of the previously stacked GO nanosheets by strong electrostatic attraction force or acid-base reaction to form a CQD-intercalated GOM structure. After further water-draining for another 20 min by filtration, the CQD-intercalated GOM, represented by x-CQD_GOM, was obtained and transferred to a silica gel-containing desiccator for reservation for at least one night before use. For the use of characterization, self-supporting GOM and x-CQD_GOM of greater thickness were also prepared by depositing an equivalent of 12.8 times quantity of GO, or that and CQD (from 200 mL of 0.02 mg⋅L-1 GO solution and the same proportional quantity of CQD) on a smaller NCM (pore size: 0.1 μm; diameter: 90 mm). The thickness of x-CQD_GOM used for separation experiments was calculated by average using areal density of GO based on that of the self-supporting one. In addition, the control molecular EDA intercalated GOM, represented by EDA_GOM, was prepared using the same procedure above except for that 100 mL of 12.2 mM EDA solution was used in the second step instead of the CQD solution. The equivalent amount of the EDA used was highly excessive as compared to the conventional cationic exchange capacity (3 ∼ 6 mmol∙g−1) of GO for assuring sufficient ion-exchange or acid-base reaction of EDA with GO surface. The MB-pre-adsorbed GOMs with saturated and a half saturated MB adsorption, represented by MB1_GOM and MB0.5_GOM, respectively, were prepared by filtrating 100 mL and 50 mL of 5×10-4 g∙L-1 of MB solution, respectively, through a prepared GOM, following which the membranes were continuously rinsed with MilliQ water for 24 h and dried in a silica gel-contained desiccator overnight before further use.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the wet slurry samples and the dried self-supporting membrane samples, that is, those before and after drying in the silica gel-contained desiccator, were measured using a Rigaku Smartlab diffractometer (Cu tube, λ(kα) = 0.15418 nm) at 40 V and 30 mA. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and Raman spectroscopies were recorded on a PerkinElmer spectrum 100 Optica ATR-FTIR spectrometer and a JASCO NRS-4500-type spectrometer, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using a ULVAC-PHI 5000 Versa Probe instrument with Al Kα irradiation. Field-emission scanning electron microscopic (FE-SEM) images of GOM were obtained using a Hitachi S-4300 microscope at a 3 kV acceleration voltage. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained using a JEOL JEM-2100F-type transmission electron microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The self-supporting membranes were used as the observation objects and observations were made from the top view of thinner parts of the torn membrane pieces. Zeta potential was recorded on an ELSZ-1000 Zeta-potential & Particle size Analyzer. The excitation-emission-matrix (EEM) spectra were measured by a Horiba Co.-made Aqualog type fluorescence instrument. Quantum yield of CQDs was measured using a Hamamatsu Co.-made C11347-type absolute PL quantum yield spectrometer.

The removal performance of the fabricated GO and x-CQD_GO membranes was evaluated using a laboratory-scale cross-flow filtration system. The detailed apparatus set-up, procedure, conditions, and analyzing methods were referred to in Supplementary Materials. Rejections (r) of methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), and 37 kinds of PPCPs (Table S1) and the corresponding water fluxes (Jw ) were calculated through the following equations:
(1)

%
=
C 0
-
C e
/
C 0
× 100
(2)
J w
( L ·
h

·
m
- 2
· b a
r

)
= F /
( A × P )
where C
0 and Ce are feed and permeate concentrations, respectively, F the permeate flowrate, A the effective filtration area of GOM, and P the hydraulic pressure of water. Adsorption amount of dye molecules after 2 h operation (q) was calculated by the following equation and was used to do comparison among the membrane materials:
(3)
q
mmol ·
g
- 1
=
C i
-
C
0 , 2
×
V 0
/ W / M
where C
i , C
0, 2 , V
0 , W, and M are the initial feed concentration, the feed concentration after 2 h operation, the total water volume (350 mL), GO amount in GOM (238.8 μg [41]), and molecular weight, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the ordinary GOM of negatively charged surface repels negatively charged species but attracts positively charged ones, achieving a separation effect by electrostatic exclusion and by adsorption, respectively. The latter mechanism often causes membrane blockade and has an issue of regeneration for the sake of repeating use because of saturation adsorption limitation. The idea of this work is to create an interlayer environment of GOM which accommodates both negatively and positively charged surface groups for realizing an electrostatic repulsion effect toward not only negatively but also positively charged filtrate species. This membrane structure is certainly also effective for the rejection of hydrophobic filtrate species because of the incompatible polarity (hydrophobicity vs hydrophilicity) repulsion effect.

To aim at this goal by CQD intercalation, the key point is to gain an appropriate CQD which has sufficient quantity of positively charged surface groups such that some of them can remain free or spare in interlayers even after acid-base or ion-exchange intercalation reaction with the internal surface of GO. A simple and comparatively mild hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs were then attempted using sucrose as the precursor under chemical environments applying various alkaline additives. Fig. 2
a shows the pH dependency of zeta potentials of GO and various carbon particles obtained. Because of the photoluminescent property and weak scattering intensity on the consequence of the small size, it was not possible to directly measure zeta-potentials of the CQDs. Here the surface properties of the CQDs were judged from those of their long-reaction and thus largely grown nanoparticle products by considering that they have the similar property in terms of surface charging (as will be confirmed in the FT-IR results below). The bare sucrose-derived Suc(3 h)-CP displays a negative zeta-potential value in a wide pH range similar to GO, characteristic of negatively charged surface. However, coexistence of alkaline additives tends to shift the zeta potential of CPs to positive side. Particularly, EDA addition changes the surface charge to be positive at pH < 7, leading to a stable colloidal solution having a zeta potential value over + 30 mV. It is thus expected that EDA- CQD can be an ideal counterpart material with GOM for the purpose of forming a diverse interlayer charging environment by intercalation.

FT-IR results (Fig. 2 b) demonstrates that Suc-CQD is actually the polymerization product of sucrose, partially imprinting the surface structure of sucrose as shown by the vibrational peaks around 3600 ∼ 3200, 2941, and 1072 ∼ 1022 cm−1 assigned to various –OH groups, CHx group, and various epoxy groups, respectively. Besides, the more evident vibrational peak at 1638 cm−1 and appearance of a shoulder peak at 2888 cm−1 are attributed to C = C stretching and –CH group, respectively, indicating formation of non-saturated carbon structure. Signals around 1100 ∼ 1400 cm−1 and those around 3639 and 1690 cm−1 becomes obvious with increasing treatment time ( Fig. S2 ), implying formation and growth of a variety of hydroxyl groups and the –COOH group, respectively, which serve as dissociative groups and determine the negatively charged surface property (i.e., a great negative value of zeta potential in Fig. 2 a). On contrary, EDA-CQD also imprints partial structure of the original sucrose, retaining vibrational peaks at 1022 cm−1 due to epoxy group. However, signals due to the nitrogen-containing groups such as those around 3351, 1602, and 1503 cm−1 for C-N, C = N, and NH groups, respectively, becomes clearly identified, indicating formation of the dominant nitrogen-related groups. These groups further grow with treatment time ( Fig. S2 ), evidencing that they are the main surface groups responsible for the positively charged surface property appearing in Fig. 2 a. As compared to Suc-CQD, EDA-CQD presents a more evident peak due to CH at 2908 cm−1 and its epoxy group-derived peak changes from multi peaks to a single peak around 1022 cm−1. This means that EDA-CQD is polymerized to a greater extent so as to form a greater aromatic or non-saturated cyclic structure and thus a simpler chemical environment for epoxy group as compared to Suc-CQD. Fig. 2 c shows the UV–Vis spectrum of EDA-CQD in comparison with that of Suc-CQD. In contrast to a sole weak absorption peak around 280 nm for Suc-CQD, EDA-CQD has an additional absorption peak around 390 nm whose excitation induces a luminescent peak around 503 nm, emitting green light as demonstrated in the inset photograph. This is indicative of the quantum dot property of the obtained water-soluble EDA-CQD with plenty of SFGs. Fig. 2 d shows the excitation-emission-matrix (EEM) spectrum of EDA-CQD which displays a three dimensional contour map of photoluminescent intensity with the summit centered at the excitation of 390 nm and emission of 500 nm, and the excitation wavelength ranging from ∼ 300 to ∼ 450 nm and that of emission wavelength from ∼ 380 to ∼ 600 nm. The much greater absorption of EDA-CQD as compared to Suc-CQD is attributed to more mature polymeric structure, which results in a photogenic quantum yield of ∼ 6% greater by about 2 times than that of Suc-CQD (∼3%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD, SEM and TEM of CQD-intercalated GOM
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the positively charged surface property, the EDA-CQDs can be retained in between the previously stacked GO nanosheets to form the EDA-CQD-intercalated GOM structure in the second filtration step of the membrane fabrication process. Elctrostatic attraction force leading to ion-exchange or acid-base reaction is considered as the driving force for their strong fastening on GO surface. Here the EDA-CQD-intercalated GOM structure was first analyzed by XRD measurements and microscopic observations. Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of the bare and EDA, EDA-CQD-intercalated GOMs and a depiction of the change in (001) spacing (d001 ) of the membrane samples before and after drying. From the expansion of interlayer distance of the dried samples (Fig. 3 a), it can be judged that EDA and EDA-CQD are successfully intercalated in GOM despite that the (001) diffraction peak becomes broader for EDA-CQD_GOM, indicating a less ordered layer structure. The increase in d100 of EDA-CQD_GOM as compared to the bare GOM is quite close to the backbone thickness of the CQD (∼0.3 nm, an atomic size of carbon), meaning that the CQD is possibly intercalated with the two-dimensional backbone plane parallel to the GO layer surface. It is well-known that GO changes its d001 in the range of ∼ 0.6 to ∼ 1.3 nm from the completely dehydrated state to that of saturated hydration depending on water contents. The decrease of d001 value from the wet to the dried GOMs (Fig. 3 b) (∼0.47 nm, the equivalent of loss of ∼ two layers of water molecules) means the decrease in water content of GOM from almost complete saturation to a medium hydrated state. Such changes in d001 values before and after drying (Δd100 ) can then be used as a rough indicator and were shown in Fig. 3 c to compare the degree of interlayer hydrophilicity of the GOMs. It is obvious that Δd001 of EDA-CQD_GOM (0.479 nm) is almost the same as that of the bare GOM (0.475 nm) to the contrast of which the Δd001 value of EDA_GOM (0.367 nm) is much smaller. Therefore, intercalation of small EDA molecules offsets polar sites on GO surface by electrical neutralization and decreases the hydrophilicity of GOM interlayers. On contrary, a large quantity of the spare reversely charged functional groups can be donated in GOM interlayers by EDA-CQD intercalation, making it possible to maintain an unchanged hydrophilic interlayer environment even though partial polar sites on GO surface are neutralized.

Fig. 4 shows the FE-SEM images of EDA-CQD_GOM in comparison with that of GOM. Both surfaces of GOM and EDA-CQD_GOM are very rough due to gathering of the curled layer surface and edges (Fig. 4 a ∼ f). However, that of EDA-CQD_GOM looks more “patterned”, presenting cell-like linkage structure typically with a flatter center area and an evident protruded edge boundary (Fig. 4 b ∼ e). The unique top view image is resulted from the strong electrostatic attraction between the intercalated EDA-CQD and GO layers which tends to closely stick together the GO layers on both sides of the EDA-CQDs. Fig. 4 g shows the cross-sectional image of the thicker self-supporting EDA- CQD_GOM prepared at a condition of greater quantities of GO and EDA-CQD with a smaller membrane area. From the thickness of the self-supporting EDA-CQD_GOM (∼1 μm), that of the EDA-CQD_GOM used for separation experiment was estimated to be ∼ 23 nm by average using areal density of GO. Fig. 5 compares the TEM images of EDA-CQD_GOM with that of GOM. In contrast to that GOM presents a “clean” surface view containing some denser structures due to the uprising edges of GO layers (Fig. 5 c), indeed a lot of additional dotted structures can be identified from the image of EDA-CQD_GOM, which distribute across the whole membrane area (Fig. 5 a). From the expanded images (Fig. 5 b), it becomes possible to recognize the multi-scale sized (from 10 to ∼ 30 nm in the image) and two-dimensional shaped CQDs. Some ambiguous lattice fringes can be observed from the images of the CQDs, suggesting formation of some desaturated carbon structure (C = C bond or aromatic linkage) in their structure. The larger lateral size (10 to ∼ 30 nm) confirms that the CQDs are only able to be intercalated with their two-dimensional backbone plane parallel to the GO surface as discussed in the above XRD result. Therefore, the XRD, SEM and TEM results demonstrated the formation of an EDA-CQD intercalated GO structure in EDA-CQD_GOM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RAMAN, FT-IR, and XPS of CQD-intercalated GOM
ce:section.ce:para:
To further gain an insight into the interlayer structure of the CQD-intercalated GOM, RAMAN, FT-IR, and XPS techniques were applied. Fig. 6
a compares the RAMAN spectra of GOM, EDA-CQD_GOM, and EDA(3 h)-CP. Besides the ordinary D, G, 2D and D + G bands of GO structure at 1331, 1598, 2662, and 2929 cm−1, respectively, vibrational bands from N-containing groups such as those at 1624, 3257, and 3840 cm−1 due to pyridine/pyrrolic ring stretching, CH stretching of pyridine/pyrrole, and NH stretching of pyrrole, respectively, can be obviously observed for EDA-CQD_GOM. The uprising baseline with the increase of wavenumber is indicative of the photoluminescence effect, further evidencing the existence of CQD-like particles in EDA-CQD_GOM. From FT-IR results (Fig. 6 b), the surface groups of GO are identified as carboxy, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups with characteristic peaks at 3567 (–OH stretching) and 1736 (C = O) cm−1, around 1200 ∼ 1400 cm−1, and at 1036 cm−1, respectively. Bands related to N-contained cyclic desaturated structure such as CH vending at 1063 cm−1, CH bending at 1356 cm−1, C = C group at 1578 cm−1, CH stretching at 3129 cm−1, and NH stretching at 3400 cm−1 were clearly found for both EDA_GOM and EDA-CQD_GOM. Thus, both RAMAN and FT-IR spectra confirm the successful inclusion of EDA and EDA-CQD in GOM by the filtration method.

From the C 1 s XPS result in Fig. 6 c, GO is confirmed to have 47.6 mol% C-OH/C-O groups and 5.2 mol% COOH group at 286.7 and 288.5 eV, respectively, over 47.3 mol% C = C/C-C structure at 284.6 eV, to the contrast of which Suc-CQD only holds 11.7 mol% of COOH group at 288.5 eV as the main SFG over 64.0 mol% C = C and 24.3 mol% C-C structures at 284.6 and 285.7 eV, respectively. It is as expected that EDA-CQD contains large quantity of C-N groups (63.4 mol% at 286.2 eV), certain quantity of C = C (11.3 mol% at 284.6 eV) and C = O (22.5 mol% at 287.6 eV) groups, and little quantity of COOH/COO groups (2.9 mol% at 288.7 eV). The signal of C-OH/C-O groups on GO was found to be reduced in intensity for EDA-CQD_GOM, indicating that hydroxyl group mainly participates in reaction with the N-containing group of EDA-GQD so that the CQD can be fastened on the surface of GO. From the N 1 s XPS signal (Fig. 6 d), the N-containing groups of EDA-GQD consists of 69.3 mol% of pyrrole group at 400 eV, 18.2 mol% of pyridine group at 398.7 eV, and 12.5 mol% of quaternary nitrogen group at 401.8 eV. The pyridine signal of EDA-CQD disappears after forming EDA-CQD_GOM, indicating that it is the more basic pyridine species that interacts with the acidic –OH or –COOH on GO by certain type of chemical reactions such as acid-base reaction to make the CQD strongly localized on the GO surface. The disappearance of the pyridine signal is attributed to the blue shift of the B. E. after the formation of chemical bonding, which overlaps with that of pyrrole species. Therefore, it is truly clear that EDA-CQD is intercalated in GOM in such a manner that it is strongly fastened on GO surface through the pyridine species with a large part of pyrrole species remaining “free” so that positively charged groups are able to be donated in interlayers of GOM. In other words, as a part of acidic –OH or COOH species can survive on GO surface in EDA-CQD_GOM due to geometric constraints, they can be dissociated in water, contributing to negatively charged surface, while on the other hand their dissociated protons react with pyrrole groups of EDA-CQD to form positively charged groups. By this way, an ideal interlayer charging environment coexisting with both negatively and positively charged groups is created.

The representative anionic MO and cationic MB dyes were first used to evaluate the separation property of EDA-CQD-intercalated GOM. Fig. 7 compares the rejection (r%) and water flux (JW ) for GOM, EDA_GOM, and EDA-CQD_GOM. As already reported, GOM increases rejections toward MO and MB with the increase of operation times due to surface deposition and interlayer adsorption, respectively, but at the cost of a great decrease in water flux [41]. On contrary, the water flux of EDA_GOM after long-term (24 h) operation (0.9 and 0.59 L∙h-1∙m-2∙bar−1 for MO and MB, respectively) was greatly improved as compared to that of GOM (0.51 and 0.28 L∙h-1∙m-2∙bar−1 for MO and MB, respectively), which is attributed to the expanded interlayer distance of the intercalated structure. While MO rejection by EDA_GOM (93%) is comparable with that by GOM (90%) and keeps unchanged after a long term operation, MB rejection by EDA_GOM (90%) is a little smaller than that of GOM (95%) and slightly decreased to 85% after 24 h. This is indicative of the losing of adsorption site due to the offset of negatively charged groups in the GOM interlayers by EDA intercalation in consistence with the monitored adsorption amount results of MB (1.28 and 1.39 mmol∙g−1 for EDA_GOM and the bare GOM, respectively). Reduction in adsorption makes it easier for MB to penetrate through the membrane. In comparison with other two samples, EDA-CQD_GOM keeps a high rejection toward both MO (93%) and MB (93%) and has the highest water fluxes (1.0 and 0.73 L∙h-1∙m-2∙bar−1 for MO and MB, respectively) after 24 h operation. While MO adsorption on EDA-CQD_GOM (0.2 mmol∙g−1) was still as little as that on the bare GOM (0.21 mmol∙g−1), MB adsorption on EDA-CQD_GOM (0.68 mmol∙g−1) was reduced to less than a half of that on the bare GOM. Thus, the result demonstrates that electrostatic repulsion becomes the important separation mechanism for EDA-CQD_GOM despite the largest expanded interlayer distance (Fig. 3 a).

Fig. 8 shows the rejections of 37 kinds of PPCPs by the bare GOM and EDA-CQD_GOM, from which the critical role of positively and negatively charges-coexisting interlayer environment of GOM can be more clarified. These PPCPs are divided into three groups, i.e., the negatively charged (blue circle in Fig. 8a, b), neutral (yellow rhomboid in Fig. 8a, b), and positively charged (red squares in Fig. 8a, b) groups whose detailed physical and chemical properties were shown in Table S1 . For the bare GOM (Fig. 8 a), the negatively charged PPCPs shows the greatest rejection (>∼80%) due to electrostatic repulsion force exerted from the negatively charged surface of GO. Neutral PPCPs belong to the second group having a medium rejection value between 20 and 80%. It is not hard to understand that they are excluded because contributions from their hydrophobic carbon moieties in their structure, which are incompatible with the hydrophilic interlayer environment of GO, become more significant. These hydrophobic carbon moieties include aromatic, pirazolac, uracil/imidazole, tetracyclic, pyrimidine(pyrimido)/piperidine, alkyl/alkoxy pyridazine, and thiazole rings and others. To the contrast, it is beyond expectation that only a few positively charged PPCPs were found to have a rejection value while a lot of others either were not detected or were detected with an elevated concentration (a concentration value greater than the feed ones) in the effluent solution. This means that a lot of positively charged PPCPs were significantly adsorbed in GO interlayer through electrostatic attraction because of the negatively charged surface property of GO layers. As the result, these PPCPs are either retained in interlayers of the GOM or flew out of the GOM by a so-called “overshoot” flow-through effect [60], where the concentration levels are greater than those at the entry point into the system, during the separation period. The situation resembles that of cationic dye such as MB where adsorption is greatly involved in separation.

On the other hand, not only rejections of the negatively charged PPCPs were slightly elevated, but also rejections of the neutral PPCPs were significantly improved for EDA-CQD_GOM having a mixed negatively and positively charging interlayer environment, as compared to the bare GOM (Fig. 8 b, c). Furthermore, it is interesting that rejections of all the positively charged PPCPs becomes recognizable and are remarkably high (>56%) for EDA-CQD_GOM (Fig. 8 b, c), indicating that the main separation mechanism was changed from adsorption to electrostatic repulsion. Therefore, it is the diverse charging interlayer environment that drives electrostatic repulsion toward both negatively and positively charged PPCPs. Meanwhile, as the interlayer environment is also hydrophilic, EDA-CQD_GOM can exclude neutral PPCPs whose hydrophobic structure moieties contribute more in their polarity. Thus, EDA-CQD_GOM is peculiar of such a distinctly charged and hydrophilic interlayer environment and can reject over 56% of all the PPCPs. It is further remarkable that rejection slightly increases with the increase of molecular weight roughly in a linear relationship for neutral and positively charged PPCPs (Fig. 8 b). This manifests that the role of either hydrophobicity or molecular size gradually becomes profound for the two groups of PPCPs, particularly, the neutral PPCPs (showing a higher slope) for EDA-CQD_GOM.

To further certificate that offset of interlayer charges has no such a separation effect, separation behaviors of the PPCPs on MB-pre-adsorbed GOM were examined. As a kind of typical cationic dye, MB can be strongly adsorbed on GO surface [41]. Its adsorption is confirmed to be irreversible to the degree that no desorption was observed from the MB-pre-adsorbed GOM after pure water rinsing at room temperature over 24 h. Thereby, MB-pre-adsorbed GOM can be considered as the GOM whose interlayers are bridged by molecular MB which neutralizes the negative charges on GO surface and thus offsets a certain amount of interlayer charges depending upon the MB adsorption amount. Fig. 9 compares the proportional ratio of the rejection of EDA-CQD_GOM (rEDA-CQD_GOM), that of MB-pre-adsorbed GOM with a half of MB saturation adsorption (rMB0.5_GOM), and that of MB-pre-adsorbed GOM with saturation adsorption of MB (rMB1_GOM) over that of the bare GOM (rGOM). It is obvious that, reflecting the high PPCPs separation property, the rEDA-CQD_GOM/rMB1_GOM ratio of the EDA-CQD_GOM is an enhancing factor with a value over unity for all the PPCPs, and is very great for neutral and positively charged PPCPs and much greater particularly for those with a greater molecular weight. However, proportional ratios of the MB-pre-adsorbed GOM becomes a reduction factor which decreases with the increase of the MB pre-adsorption amount and becomes almost zero for most of the PPCPs for MB1_GOM. This result clearly shows that offset of interlayer charges has nothing to do with the increase of rejections by electrostatic repulsion but works for losing of affinity obstacles which helps the penetration of all the PPCPs.

An overall rejection effect toward PPCPs of various polarities can be achieved by controlling interlayer charging environment of GOM through intercalation of CQD. CQD derived from the mixture of sucrose and EDA was found to be the appropriate intercalant candidate because of its rich positively charged surface groups and thin two-dimensional geometry. While pyridine groups of EDA-CQD serve as anchors to fasten the CQD onto GO surface, pyrrole groups on the CQD and the left hydroxyl groups on GO surface function as proton receiver and donor, respectively, thus enabling an interlayer environment coexisting with both positively and negatively charged groups in GOM. Such a peculiar CQD-intercalated GOM structure improves water fluidity because of the slightly expanded galley height and achieves a high-efficient all-together rejection effect toward PPCPs of all polarities (from null rejection by the bare GOM to all more than 56% rejection) due to electrostatic expulsion and polarity expelling. The research result presented here is the first example of changing chemical environment of a membrane material to totally remove numerous PPCPs of different polarities and paves a new way for designing future simpler membrane materials other than nanofiltration and reverse osmosis.

M. O. and R. K. conducted this work under the professorship of partner graduate school between National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST) and Chiba Institute of Technology (CIT) and are indebted to AIST for technical assistance and financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.144811.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
